author: @PetraKann
text:
The first 2 laws in Science were derived by Archimedes: The Law of Levers and the Law of Buoyancy.

The important thing to remember about Ohm’s Law is that Temperature is constant. So V=IR only holds at a specific temperature.
You can include the effect of Temperature in an Ohmic conductor by knowing the reference resistance Rref, the reference temperature and the value of “α” and then applying the following relationship that with give you the resistance at any temperature (apart from super conductors):

R=Rref [1+α(T-Tref)]

Then of course there are non-Ohmic conductors: ie where the relationship between voltage and current is not linear. V=IR does not apply or hold for these conductors or devices.

A non-linear relationship can be used to predict a value for the apparent resistance at a given potential difference or voltage. That is resistance varies with voltage

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like the way you put that mate - derived - nice - and you are spot on about resistance and have have covered that with Luke but thanks for pointing it out for everyone. But the real mistake I made here was having two teaching points - should only be one teaching point in a lesson

replies.author: @PetraKann
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Cheers from Australia.
There are different types of "laws" in science. Some are more fundamental, some are more empirical or analytical.
In the end they are really, as you say descriptive in nature. They help us understand what is happening, what the variables are, what assumptions were made to get to these laws and their limitations. A good example is Einstein's General Relativity equation - beautifully descriptive and super-accurate, yet they collapse at a singularity and dont apply or become non-sensical with all sort of infinities arising. Same thing with Quantum Mechanics.
Perhaps the most fundamental of all Scientific laws that have stood the test of time of several centuries, and have no refuting evidence from observation or experiment are the 4 laws of Thermodynamics.
They say that it doesnt really matter how good your theory looks or how good the inventor is, if the theory violates the Laws of Thermodynamics, you better start again or at least look at the derivation again.

Ohm's law for super-conductors means that the Resistance is zero which implies that if you apply a voltage at one end of the super-conductor, the voltage will not change across the entire length of the conductor ie Zero voltage drop. So what is the current flow in such a superconductor?

author: @SlyerFox666
text: Nice, The triangle is useful in a few ways  One to remember and use Ohms law, we were taught to just cover up the one you want to work it out. So cover V your left with I x R or cover R and your left with V/I.  Two if you put the P (for Power measured in Watts), in place of the V and scoot the letters round anti clockwise and drop off the R you now have P over V x I easy way to remember the power triangle, and use it just the same. Three if you can understand how to use the triangle you now understand the basics of transposition in maths, Give yourself a pat on the back 👍... Great for teachers and learners.

author: @larryniidji
text: Mechanical equivalents. Electricity and water have many parallels. Both seek the lowest point. The hose or pipe diameter are the resistance (R). The PSI of water, the pressure is your volts (E) and the amount of water per period of time, gallons or liters per minute are the current (I). The faucet is a variable resistor while a diaphragm in the hose or pipe is a capacitor. In some regards a container of water can act as a capacitor (only in the energy storage sense). The inductor I see as the coiled pipe or tube being elastic so as to limit flow (I) control as pressure (E) builds, it expands and then to also resist change as pressure is reduced, it shrinks (returns the energy of maintaining flow (I) control).

author: @stevetobias4890
text: The rock, the hill and gravity. Ohm's Law should be known as ohm's principal (then add in some watts). It is an electrical calculation that helps us understand the relationship between resistance, voltage and current. By the way, the stone will continue to roll as long as it has momentum, once it no longer has gravity acting on it it will remain still unless you get out your oxen to move it out of the way. I've noticed that there are no principles (calculations) to describe tides and the relationship to waves. 😅

author: @TheBaconWizard
text:
There's a serious and important disconnect between scientific and professional language, and everyday language. It needs to be fixed because in many cases it is very confusing and leads to some very damaging ideas, and of course unscrupulous actors sometimes deliberately use it for their own gain; social, political, financial...

The idea that a scientific law is something you can rebel against seems amusing but in the American Dream type context can be frightening. 

Try explaining to people that a "Theory" in science is verrrry different from a theory in common parlance, it is NOT just a neet story or idea that seems to fit the facts. IMO we should call The Theory of Evolution the LAW of evolution, for example, but as you show here, perhaps that too is not an accurate discription.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I see that disconnect all the time mate and the consequence is serious misunderstanding

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: Resistance was just a choice. They could easily have picked conductivity for ohms law ( C=1/R ) in which case I=U*C would have been the formula we all worked with. And if you wanted the replacement conductivity for 2 resistors in parallel you could just add the conductivities together. For two resistors in series it would be more complicated. Aint that fun ....
